functions.name: comma
functions.doc: The default RFC 4180 dialect: comma-delimited.

functions.name: tab
functions.doc: Tab-separated values.

functions.name: with_delimiter
functions.doc:
A dialect with an arbitrary single-character delimiter. A
delimiter that is not exactly one character, or that is the
quote character itself, is a programming error the parser
rejects with `BadDialect` rather than misbehaving silently.

functions.name: error_message
functions.doc:
A human-readable, backend-identical rendering of an error.
Positions are 1-based record numbers, the unit an operator
reasons about.

functions.name: parse
functions.doc: Parse CSV text with the default comma dialect.

functions.name: parse_with
functions.doc: Parse CSV text with an explicit dialect.

args: BadDialect("delimiter must be exactly one character, got '${...}'")
"delimiter must be exactly one character, got '${...}'"
BadDialect("delimiter cannot be the quote character")
BadDialect("delimiter cannot be a newline character")

functions.name: parse_headed
functions.doc:
Parse CSV text and take its first record as the header.
Empty input yields an empty header and no rows.

functions.name: parse_headed_with
functions.doc: Parse with an explicit dialect, first record as the header.

functions.name: from_table
functions.doc:
Build a header view from an already-parsed Table. The first
record becomes the columns; the rest become the data rows.

functions.name: length
functions.doc: The number of data records (header excluded).

functions.name: column_index
functions.doc: The zero-based index of a column, or None if absent.

functions.name: has_column
functions.doc: Whether the header declares a column by this name.

functions.name: get
functions.doc:
The value of `column` in data record `row_index`
(zero-based, header excluded). Errors with `NoSuchColumn`
when the header has no such column, with `NoSuchRow` when
there is no data record at `row_index`, and with `ShortRow`
when the record exists but has fewer fields than the
column's index.

args: Record { name: name, email: email, national_id: national_id, iban: iban, regi...
Bucket { key: b.key, count: b.count + 1, total_cents: b.total_cents + amount }
Bucket { key: key, count: 1, total_cents: amount }

functions.name: _capa_m10__hex_digit
functions.doc: One lowercase hex character for a nibble in 0..15.

functions.name: to_hex
functions.doc: Encode a byte list as a lowercase hex String, two chars per byte.

functions.name: _capa_m9__rotr32
functions.doc:
Rotate a 32-bit word right by `n` bits. `x` is assumed already
masked to 32 bits; `(x << (32 - n))` can reach 2^63 only if x had
high bits, so the whole expression is re-masked to 32 bits.

functions.name: _capa_m9__shr32
functions.doc: Logical right shift of an already-masked 32-bit word.

functions.name: _capa_m9__round_constants
functions.doc:
The 64 round constants (first 32 bits of the fractional parts of
the cube roots of the first 64 primes).

functions.name: _capa_m9__iv256
functions.doc: SHA-256 initial hash value.

functions.name: _capa_m9__iv224
functions.doc: SHA-224 initial hash value.

functions.name: _capa_m9__read_word
functions.doc:
Read one 32-bit big-endian word from `block` starting at byte `i`.
Each byte is in 0..255; the top byte is masked to 8 bits before
the 24-bit shift so the product stays well under i64.

functions.name: _capa_m9__schedule
functions.doc: Build the 64-word message schedule for one 64-byte block.

functions.name: _capa_m9__compress
functions.doc:
One block of the compression function. Returns the updated state
(the eight working words added back into the incoming state).

functions.name: _capa_m9__pad
functions.doc:
SHA padding: append 0x80, then zeros, then the 64-bit big-endian
bit length, to a multiple of 64 bytes. Returns a fresh byte list.

functions.name: _capa_m9__digest_words
functions.doc:
Run the full hash over `message` starting from `iv`, returning the
eight 32-bit state words.

functions.name: _capa_m9__slice64
functions.doc: Copy a 64-byte block starting at `start`.

functions.name: _capa_m9__words_to_bytes
functions.doc: Serialize `count` state words as big-endian bytes (4 each).

functions.name: sha256_bytes
functions.doc:
SHA-256 digest of `message` as 32 raw bytes.
`message`: bytes, each in 0..255; out-of-range elements are
silently masked to 8 bits (`& 0xFF`). See the BYTE CONTRACT above.

functions.name: sha256_hex
functions.doc:
SHA-256 digest of `message` as a 64-character lowercase hex String.
`message`: bytes, each in 0..255; out-of-range elements are
silently masked to 8 bits (`& 0xFF`). See the BYTE CONTRACT above.

functions.name: sha256_hex_utf8
functions.doc: SHA-256 of a String's UTF-8 bytes, as lowercase hex.

functions.name: sha224_bytes
functions.doc:
SHA-224 digest of `message` as 28 raw bytes (the first seven
state words; the eighth is dropped).
`message`: bytes, each in 0..255; out-of-range elements are
silently masked to 8 bits (`& 0xFF`). See the BYTE CONTRACT above.

functions.name: sha224_hex
functions.doc:
SHA-224 digest of `message` as a 56-character lowercase hex String.
`message`: bytes, each in 0..255; out-of-range elements are
silently masked to 8 bits (`& 0xFF`). See the BYTE CONTRACT above.

functions.name: sha224_hex_utf8
functions.doc: SHA-224 of a String's UTF-8 bytes, as lowercase hex.

functions.name: _capa_m8__key_block
functions.doc:
Normalize a key to exactly 64 bytes: hash it if it is longer than
the block, then zero-pad to the block size.

functions.name: _capa_m8__xor_pad
functions.doc: XOR every byte of a 64-byte block with a constant pad byte.

functions.name: _capa_m8__concat
functions.doc: Concatenate two byte lists into a fresh list.

functions.name: hmac_sha256_bytes
functions.doc:
HMAC-SHA256 of `message` under `key`, as 32 raw bytes.
BYTE CONTRACT: each element of `key` and `message` must be in
0..255. Out-of-range elements are silently masked to 8 bits
(`& 0xFF`: 256 -> 0, -1 -> 255), with no error, identically on
both backends. For text, prefer the `_utf8` wrapper or
`String.bytes()`, which are always in range.

functions.name: hmac_sha256_hex
functions.doc:
HMAC-SHA256 of `message` under `key`, as lowercase hex.
BYTE CONTRACT: each element of `key` and `message` must be in
0..255; out-of-range elements are silently masked to 8 bits
(`& 0xFF`), with no error, identically on both backends.

functions.name: hmac_sha256_hex_utf8
functions.doc:
HMAC-SHA256 over the UTF-8 bytes of a String key and message, as
lowercase hex.

args: "GDPR Art. 4(5) pseudonymisation: the public report references a subject only...

declassifications.reason: GDPR Art. 4(5) pseudonymisation: the public report references a subject only by a keyed one-way token of the email, from which the email cannot be recovered; the direct identifier never leaves the secret domain

args: "no raw personal data reached the public report (proved by capa --check)"

operator_declared_grants.note: Authority declared by the operator at build/run time (e.g. --preopen). DISTINCT from the compiler-derived, program-proven capability surface; the compiler could not derive these grants.

compiler_derived_path_arg_surface.note: argv (env.args()) arguments the compiler PROVED reach an Fs / Net / Env sink, and whether read or write. A sound over-approximation (no reaching argument omitted; arg_index '*' when the concrete index is not statically determinate). Closures are covered SOUND BY CONSTRUCTION: a closure whose parameter reaches a sink is reported at '*' UNLESS the compiler proves it is applied only locally to non-argv values -- so a closure that escapes its frame (returned, passed to a helper, stored in an aggregate, or reached through a match/if arm or a name bound to one) fails closed, with no per-shape enumeration. Scope is EXHAUSTIVELY covered: the statement traversal descends into EVERY sub-scope that holds sub-statements -- if/while/for statement blocks, match-arm blocks, and lambda bodies, at any nesting depth -- so a binding or sink in any sub-block (including a match-arm block) is never skipped (pinned by a meta-test over the AST node inventory). RESIDUAL (the one honest gap, VALUE-FLOW only): a closure carried by a value the AST-level pass cannot statically tie back to a lambda -- re-extracted from a runtime container by key, or threaded through an opaque computed value -- may be under-reported. This does NOT grant authority -- a dynamic Fs/Net path still fail-closes in --wasi without --preopen; it is an auditable fact, distinct from operator_declared_grants.
